data.title: Y-mAbs Therapeutics: Slow But Steady Prospect In Rare Cancers
data.description:
Y-mAbs Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:YMAB) has one drug approved in two indications, with a late-stage pipeline for that same drug in 2-3 other indications, and an early-stage player called SADA which is basically a delivery mechanism for radio immunotherapies. In this last one, the company says they're “currently in Part A of our GD2-SADA Phase I trial, evaluating the safety and tolerability of the SADA PRIT in the treatment of GD2-positive solid tumors, including small cell lung cancer, sarcomas, and malignant melanoma.” On this note, I will not focus on SADA because there's no efficacy data yet.

Danyelza and its current status

Danyelza, scientific name naxitamab, is approved in combination, with granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor (GM-CSF) for R/R High-risk neuroblastoma in a pediatric setting. R/R HR NB, which affects young children, has three phases of treatment - induction, consolidation, and post-consolidation. In the induction and consolidation phases, current standards of care are multiagent chemotherapy, surgical resection, and high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue. However, over 80% of patients relapse or have inadequate response. In the third stage, we now have anti-GD2 immunotherapy using dinutuximab in a first-line setting, marketed by United Therapeutics Corporation (UTHR), and naxitamab for the r/r setting.

Naxitamab has gone through a pivotal phase 2 trial called Study 201 for this accelerated approval. Interim data from this trial:

As of March 2021, 48 patients were included in the safety population and 36 in the efficacy population. The overall response rate was 58% (21/36) with a complete response rate of 44% (16/36) and a partial response rate of 14% (5/36). The median duration of response was 25 weeks. Infusion-related reactions (including pain) were the most common adverse events, occurring in all patients. Eleven patients reported 13 naxitamab-related SAEs (four hypotension, four anaphylactic reactions, and one each for fatigue, pyrexia, laryngeal edema, respiratory depression, and urticaria). Four patients (8%) discontinued naxitamab due to naxitamab-related AEs. No AEs were fatal. Anti-drug antibody formation was observed in 10/46 (22%) patients assessed.

A confirmatory trial is ongoing, and the product is already on the market. The company guided for $87-$95 million in revenue for the full-year 2024. The company estimates there are 300 patients in the 2L setting. The treatment costs around $1.2mn per patient per year, although since dosage is patient weight-based, it's difficult to pin a specific price tag.

Future prospects

Naxitamab is currently undergoing multiple trials. One of these is directed toward a frontline induction setting in the same indication, which pits it against dinutuximab directly. This is in a phase 2 trial and will considerably extend the target market for naxitamab. There are 450 more patients in this setting.

In this trial, the partner is the BEAT Childhood Cancer Research Consortium, which is leading a multi-center Phase II trial evaluating naxitamab in combination with standard induction therapy for patients with newly diagnosed HRNB. Through August, 17 sites had been opened, and 10 patients treated, with recruitment ongoing.

This trial will eventually test naxitamab vs. the current standard of care, which is chemotherapy, in a randomized trial. The aim is to demonstrate superiority in complete response rate.

A second trial is in relapsed second-line osteosarcoma, which is being run by Memorial Sloan Kettering and data is expected in Q4. Here, the update we have as of August is the following:

In late June 2024, we received a preliminary draft abstract of certain results from MSK on this trial. For the 39 patients in the study with pulmonary-only recurrence, the summary stated there were 14 event-free patients at 12 months, rather than MSK's primary endpoint of 16 event-free patients at 12 months.

Obviously, as the trial completes, there may be more event-free patients. We have no further update at this time.

One other trial is as chemo immunotherapy in r/r HRNB. This study, the company tells us, is completed.

Financials

YMAB has a market cap of $644mn and a cash balance of $78mn. Total net product revenues were $22.8 million for the quarter ended June 30, 2024, representing a 10% increase from 2023. A third of this represents international revenue. Research and development expenses were $12.3 million for the quarter ended June 30, 2024, COGS was $3mn and G&A was $17.2mn. At that rate, and taking into account the revenue stream, they have a cash runway of four to five quarters.

Risks

There are multiple investment risks here. First, the approved indication is a very small market: Even at the $1+mn annual cost, the entire market size is hardly $300mn. They have already penetrated a third of this small market, which seems like a commendable achievement. But the US efforts seem saturated already, and international revenues will be more of a volume game.

Second, their cash balance is not considerable. No doubt the revenue stream extends it somewhat, but it leaves them with little leeway to actively expand labels and do more R&D.

Bottom line

The company has started focusing a lot on SADA, and yet there's no hard data to understand this platform. The naxitamab program is generating revenue, but so far, its market potential is limited by the relatively small patient population. I still like YMAB - decent revenue stream, convincing future prospects, a new platform in testing - but its low cash situation gives me pause. I would like to watch it for a while longer before I decide to invest.

data.title: USD/JPY Technical: Potential Bearish Reversal In U.S. Dollar Strength Below 200-Day Moving Average
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

This is a follow-up analysis of our prior report "USD/JPY Technical: Recent mean reversion rebound in US dollar strength may have hit a ceiling” published on 4 October 2024. Click here for a recap.

Since our last publication, the price movements of USD/JPY have extended its rally by another 3% from its 4 October low to print an intraday high of 150.32 last Thursday, 17 October, and cleared above 149.30 medium-term resistance as highlighted.

All in all, the Japanese yen has weakened by 7.7% against the US dollar in the past four weeks from 16 September to 17 October (low to high) with three key related risk events looming this week; Japan’s flash services and manufacturing PMIs for October out on Thursday, 24 October, Tokyo’s CPI on Friday, 25 October, and the outcome of Japan’s snap general election held on Sunday, 27 October.

Right now, several technical analysis-related elements are suggesting potential signs of bullish exhaustion in the 4-week rally.

Bullish positioning has been reduced in the JPY currency futures market

Based on the latest Commitments of Traders data for the week of 14 October 2024 (compiled by Macro Micro), the aggregate net bullish open positions of large speculators in the JPY futures market (after offsetting the aggregate positions of large commercial hedgers) are at +70,749 contracts (net long), a decline of 50% in the past three weeks after it hit a 5-year high of +143,519 contracts for the week of 23 September (see Fig 1).

Net open large speculative positioning flows (primarily from hedge funds) are contrarian in nature which suggests that a relatively high level of net positioning may see an opposite reaction in price actions if related data or news flows disappoint.

Given that large speculative market participants have trimmed their net bullish open positions in the JPY currency futures market, the risk of further profit-taking activities (sell JPY, buy US dollar) has been reduced if the materialized related data or news flow does not support a bullish JPY narrative.

In contrast, if such data and news flow support a positive JPY narrative, large speculators may rebuild their net long position in the JPY futures market (buy JPY, sell US dollar) which in turn may lead to a bearish reversal in the USD/JPY.

Hovering below the 200-day moving average with exhaustion elements

The 4-week up move seen in the USD/JPY from its 16 September low of 139.58 has almost reached the key 200-day moving average that coincides closely with the 151.95 long-term pivotal resistance.

Since 8 October, the price actions of USD/JPY have taken on the form of an impending bearish “Ascending Wedge” configuration coupled with a weekly bearish “Shooting Star” candlestick pattern for the week of 14 October (see Fig 2).

Watch the 148.95 key intermediate support (close to the lower boundary of the “Ascending Wedge), and a break below it may trigger a potential bearish reversal scenario on the USD/JPY to expose the medium-term supports of 146.90 and 144.80.

On the flip side, a clearance with a daily close above 151.95 invalidates the bearish scenario for the next resistance to come in at 154.70 in the first step.

Original Post

data.title: The Monarch Cement: Resilient Leadership In A Challenging Market
data.description:
Investment thesis

The Monarch Cement (OTCPK:MCEM) is a company with a strong track record of value creation in a cyclical industry. It operates in a sector that will see significant demand in the future due to selling a low-cost product, but it will face challenges from government regulations due to the high pollution of its production process.

With high returns on capital, solid EPS growth, strong FCF, and very smart management in a tough industry, the company appears optically cheap. However, its multiples are distorted by three years of good returns on its external investments, making comparisons with peers in more reliable markets difficult.

From my point of view, the company is a great long-term investment, but it becomes unpredictable over a 5- or 10-year horizon due to the difficulty in forecasting commodity behavior. Therefore, my recommendation is a "Hold".

Introduction

Monarch Cement is a family-owned company with over 100 years of history, led by the fifth generation of the Wulf family. The company has an excellent track record of value creation, and management is known for its very prudent capital management in a highly cyclical industry like cement production and distribution.

The cement industry is one of those "boring" industries Peter Lynch appreciates, protected by its own characteristics. That is, cement demand is limited, so a region with many concentrated players becomes economically unviable. Additionally, transportation costs are high, requiring operations to be close to the areas of demand.

Business Overview

The company operates vertically, meaning it produces and distributes two types of products: cement and ready-mixed concrete.

As we can see in the following chart, in recent years, cement has come to represent a larger percentage of the total sales mix, which in 2023 consisted of 60% cement and 40% ready-mixed concrete. Another observation is that the company exhibits a certain degree of cyclicality. Although the company has shown a 6% CAGR over these 8 years, we notice stagnation and even a slight decline in 2019.

Additionally, given the revenue growth structure that I will break down later, margins have expanded significantly.

Thesis Highlights

High Insider’s ownership

The company's management and controlling owners hold around 70% of the outstanding shares in a company that does not issue new shares. The CEO and member of the founding family, Walter H. Wulf, owns 24% of the outstanding shares. Walter is a fifth-generation member of the founding family and has served as CEO since 1999. His vision is clearly focused on the long-term sustainability of the company, acting with great prudence, which extends the terminal value.

Competitive advantages

The company operates in a sector considered a natural monopoly, where cement users need to be located near their producer due to high transportation costs.

Management has been characterized by a protective approach toward the business, maintaining a very healthy financial balance, primarily aimed at keeping the company stable during crises, such as in 2008. In fact, they are so aware of the cyclical nature of the business that, during periods of high demand, they do not invest excessively in increasing production, knowing that the business is inherently cyclical.

Financial Overview

This image shows all the company's financials and their evolution over the past 6 years. As always, I consider it crucial for the company to outgrow the GDP in its operational area in order to compound our capital above the growth of the economy where it operates. This is a point that the company has met, as it has grown well above the 6% average in the USA. I believe it’s important to consider that, given the population expansion and the structural deficit in the USA, MCEM shareholders will have guaranteed sustainability of this growth.

In a brief comment on the previous chart, from my perspective, the numbers are excellent, as they show sustainable revenue growth along with a significant improvement in margins. The figures presented here may not match external platforms because I have normalized them by excluding the company's investments in other companies within the sector. I will analyze this point shortly.

The current assets consist mainly of cash and equivalents (40% of the total) and inventory (40% of the total), intended for the company's regular operations. While some materials, like gypsum or diesel fuel, have a useful life of about six months, others, such as natural gas, could potentially have an indefinite useful life. The key takeaway here is that management has consistently managed inventory correctly.

The composition of non-current assets is represented 62% by the net value of property, plant, and equipment, with only 2% as intangible assets. This not only demonstrates that the company does not focus on acquisitions, but also that all of its assets are physically verifiable.

Another important component, particularly because of its impact on the company's EPS, is the investments it holds in other companies within the industry. From my point of view, this adds greater cyclicality to the company's earnings, which I will explore further later.

Regarding liabilities, the company operates with a net cash position. This is a very solid decision by management to always counterbalance the downturns in the industry and, in the event of a major crisis, possibly make a significant acquisition to expand its economic moat.

The increase in revenue presents a very interesting mix, as it is driven by both an increase in sales volume and a rise in prices. To put this in context, in both business lines, this increase is explained 80% by price and 20% by volume.

The CapEx relative to sales has nominally increased significantly, but in percentage terms, it hasn't been as pronounced. The main issue here is that the cement industry is quite polluting, and a large portion of this CapEx is aimed at reducing toxic gas emissions, making it unclear whether it will lead to greater productivity for the company. This is a point I hope the company will explain in more detail.

The returns on capital are simply extraordinary and have been consistently expanded over time. It's always good to have a company in your portfolio with high returns on capital, but equally important is its ability to reinvest that capital, and in this regard, management is very conservative. While I believe that management is excellent at allocating this capital, the cyclical nature of the industry leads to more conservative growth plans.

Finally, I will focus on the company's EPS and how they are significantly affected by investment performance. First, I present a chart where I have normalized the company's revenues without considering the partial results of investments, net of tax effects.

As we can see, the exponential growth of normalized earnings per share has been very good. However, in the next chart, we can observe how, for example, in 2021 and 2023, the EPS has been distorted by investments, creating the illusion that the company was trading at lower valuation multiples than it actually was.

From my perspective, and especially to reduce some cyclicality in the results, it is essential to analyze the company without considering these extraordinary gains. If the investments generate good results, that's even better for us as shareholders.

Valuation

From a valuation and projections standpoint, I have decided not to delve too deeply due to the cyclicality of the industry. Therefore, I will determine whether the company is undervalued or not in comparison with its peers.

As we can see, the company appears optically undervalued based on its normalized earnings. However, I believe it is reasonable for there to be a discount compared to its peers due to two points: first, MCEM's expansion or growth plan is not entirely clear, and the company's focus is on maintaining a stable cyclical business; and second, it trades in a market with fewer regulations, such as the OTC, compared to its peers listed on the NYSE or SWX.

So, considering a reasonable P/E of 17x and an EV/EBITDA of 11x, with an annual growth rate of 7% for the company (in line with its historical average, but remembering it will be cyclical), and assuming constant EBITDA margins of 25% and a tax rate of 20%, the expected CAGR for the next 5 years is 12%.

If we consider the same assumptions but maintain the current multiples, assuming the market values the company correctly, the expected return is 7% annually until 2029.

Risks

Like any investment, it is not without risk. While it operates in a monopolistic or oligopolistic sector, depending on how we view it, the company relies heavily on very few sources of production, unlike larger competitors. This could pose a problem if any unexpected event occurs.

Another potential risk is the volatility of cement prices; if the industry faces a prolonged recession, it could lead to falling prices and, consequently, a lack of revenue to maintain the company’s profitability.

The biggest risk, from my perspective, is regulation. On one hand, the cement industry is one of the most polluting globally, so it is expected that fines or taxes on the sector will increase. On the other hand, due to the current structural shortages in the USA, it is likely that demand for this material will rise, leading the government to impose limits on price increases, thus limiting its pricing power.

Catalyst

The lack of infrastructure and housing shortages in the USA is undoubtedly the primary catalyst for the industry. Sustainable growth is expected in the coming years, and although cement production is one of the most polluting industries, it remains a crucial material for construction. While the government is constantly pushing for emission reductions, there are signs that cement is difficult to replace, which could provide considerable time for improvements in production.

Mordor Intelligence expects growth of 4.8% until 2029, which, along with a slight increase in prices, would allow us to anticipate an annual growth rate of 7%.

Conclusion

The Monarch Cement is a very interesting company in a commodities sector. Typically, these types of companies operate with significant cyclicality, and if they have a considerable level of debt, they could face bankruptcy risks. In this case, thanks to exceptional management, the company has performed excellently in a challenging industry.

For my part, while MCEM presents many characteristics aligned with my investment philosophy—such as sustained positive FCF, EPS growth, a monopolistic sector, and high returns on capital—its cyclicality prevents me from being a shareholder. At the moment, I acknowledge my limitations regarding commodities knowledge, and if I were to buy a company of this type at the peak of the cycle, it could impact my future profitability.

That said, I consider the company to be in a solid position for a very long-term investment horizon of around 20 years. Based on my investment philosophy of expecting a 15% return to maintain a margin of safety, and given that it is a cyclical company, my recommendation for now is a "Hold."

data.title: Tenable Holdings: M&A Signals Weakness
data.description:
Tenable's (NASDAQ:TENB) share price has come under pressure in recent months, with the underlying business deteriorating. This hasn't been surprising, as I previously suggested that data pointed towards weakness in Tenable's business.

While Tenable has a strong position within its core vulnerability management market, I continue to believe that it will be a loser in the shift toward unified security platforms. Acquisitions have strengthened Tenable's cloud security business, and the company has suggested that its exposure management solution is generating momentum. I don't think that this meaningfully improves the company's competitive positioning though, which may be why the company is now the target of acquisition rumors.

Market Conditions

Tenable’s commentary regarding market conditions was fairly pessimistic in the second quarter, which was somewhat at odds with most other cybersecurity vendors. Tenable suggested that customers continue to scrutinize spend and prioritize investments, which is making it difficult to close new deals, particularly larger deals. Tenable’s Vulnerability Management business has been most impacted by this. Exposure management is reportedly gaining momentum though, with customers wanting to improve their risk understanding across the entire attack surface.

Vendor consolidation and the trend toward unified security platforms are likely far more important to Tenable's more future than any near-term fluctuations in demand. Vulnerability management is a relatively small opportunity, and Tenable is trying to leverage its capabilities to expand into cloud and OT security. While there are reasons to think Tenable could have success in OT security, I ultimately expect the company's expansion efforts to struggle given the strength of competition.

Tenable has tried to position the CrowdStrike (CRWD) incident as a reason to avoid platformization. While interoperable best-of-breed solutions will remain important, I expect consolidation in the market to continue, which is problematic for Tenable, as Vulnerability Management could become more of a feature than a standalone product long-term. Not everyone holds this view though, with many expecting Exposure Management to remain a standalone product that integrates into larger platforms.

Tenable also believes that having an independent exposure assessment offers customers significant value, and I think that this is Tenable's strongest value proposition. Whether this protects the company as other cybersecurity vendors embed more vulnerability management capabilities in their platforms remains to be seen, though.

Tenable Business Updates

While exposure management continues to see strong demand, Tenable’s traditional standalone VM business is an area of weakness, with Tenable suggesting that this part of the business is becoming more cyclical. The company remains a leader in the segment though, and VM is helping to drive Tenable One (exposure management platform) adoption. Exposure management is becoming increasingly critical for customers as it provides a more complete view of risk, consolidates spend, reduces complexity and improves ROI.

Exposure management demand is being supported the adoption of AI and a desire by customers to understand where and how it is being used. Tenable's AI security posture management capabilities help customers to detect misconfigurations in their cloud services and ensure compliance with their security policies. Tenable also now offers runtime detection to identify AI software libraries and browser plug-ins.

Tenable's cloud security business also has momentum at the moment, which the company attributed to the strength of its CNAPP solution and customers prioritizing cloud security spend. Tenable has reportedly started to land technical wins with large, sophisticated enterprises and considers its solution a top five player in the market. I find this a little hard to believe given the success of CrowdStrike, Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and Wiz, along with the growing presence of companies like Fortinet (FTNT), SentinelOne (S), Check Point (CHKP) and Orca.

Tenable is trying to create a competitive CNAPP offering that helps customers identify and remediate difficult-to-detect problems. In support of this, Tenable acquired Ermetic towards the end of 2023 for $265 million. Ermetic has a cloud-native application protection platform and provides cloud infrastructure entitlement management. Ermetic was founded in 2019 and was generating around $20 million in the lead up to the acquisition. It was not all smooth sailing though, with the company laying off 17% of its workforce in early 2023. Tenable has already integrated Ermetic's platform capabilities and migrated over 1,000 customers onto the consolidated CNAPP platform.

Tenable also more recently acquired Eureka Security to add Data Security Posture Management to its platform. Tenable will help customers identify key evidence related to cloud data risk, including where sensitive data resides in the cloud, who has access to that data and the severity of the risk posed by potential data compromise. This has been a fairly hot area recently due to the rise of LLMs, with companies like Palo Alto, CrowdStrike and IBM all acquiring DSPM solutions. Tenable was reportedly only planning on spending tens of millions of dollars on Eureka, a modest price tag relative to the company's roughly $10 million trailing 12-month revenue. This acquisition brings Tenable's total spend on acquisition over the past five or so years to around $700 million.

Tenable's share price has benefitted recently from speculation that it is the target of M&A activity. The company has reportedly been exploring its options after receiving takeover interest, although it is not clear whether this was coming from a financial or strategic buyer. Given the company's current valuation, I doubt any offer would come at a large premium.

Financial Analysis

Tenable's revenue grew around 13% YoY in the second quarter to $221.2 million. Organic growth was probably closer to 10%, though. Calculated current billings (revenue + change in current deferred revenue) was $221.1 million in the second quarter, an increase of 10% YoY. Current RPO growth in the quarter was 14%.

Calculated current billings fell short of internal expectations, leading Tenable to lower its full-year outlook. The shortfall was the result of Tenable closing less deals than expected in its Vulnerability Management business. Other parts of the business performed better, with Tenable One expanding to 30% of total new enterprise sales, up from 26% in the prior quarter. Exposure solutions (including Tenable One, Cloud and OT security) represented over 50% of total new sales in the quarter.

Tenable expects $222-224 million revenue in the third quarter, an increase of roughly 11% YoY growth at the midpoint. For the full year, revenue is expected to be $889-895 million, implying around 9% YoY growth. Tenable has suggested that guidance is more conservative than normal as a result of market conditions.

Tenable added 408 net new enterprise platform customers and 76 six figure customers during Q2. The company's net dollar expansion rate was 109% and renewal and win rates remain strong.

The number of job openings mentioning Tenable in the job requirements suggests customer additions will remain weak going forward, further pressuring Tenable's growth rate.

From a profitability perspective, Tenable's business continues to look reasonably healthy. The company is generating solid cash flows, and its operating profitability is improving. Tenable also began a cost-cutting program in the fourth quarter of 2023 and some of this is now coming to fruition. The company subleased a portion of its real estate in Q2 and recognized a $4.5 million impairment charge as a result. The increase in sales and marketing expense in the quarter was driven by marketing for Tenable's cloud security and exposure management offerings.

Tenable recently introduced a $280-290 million unlevered free cash flow target for 2025. Based on this target, Tenable is only trading on around 17x next year's free cash flow. This appears to be a fairly aggressive target though (~29% free cash flow margin) and cash flows are somewhat deceptive from a profitability perspective (SBC, unearned revenue). The company also raised its long-term unlevered free cash flow target from 30% to 35%.

Conclusion

The slowdown in Tenable's business hasn't been surprising, as a range of data, like job openings, has been pointing to a vulnerability management slowdown for some time. While most of this is likely the result of a soft demand environment, it is hard not to feel that competitive pressure is also rising.

Cloud and OT security are large opportunities, which will be needed to drive growth going forward. Competition here is fierce though, particularly in cloud security, making it difficult for Tenable to establish a leadership position.

Tenable's valuation appears reasonable relative to peers, particularly given that the company is cash flow positive and should soon be consistently profitable on a GAAP basis. I view this has somewhat secondary to whether Tenable's growth is accelerating or decelerating and competitive considerations, though.

M&A rumors are also likely helping to support Tenable's share price at the moment. If nothing eventuates on this front and Tenable's growth continues to stall, the stock is likely to fall further. While an acquisition could create quick returns for shareholders, any premium is likely to be fairly modest from current levels.

data.title: Chart Of The Week: Is It This Metal's Turn To Rocket Now?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

If you’ve been paying any attention to the commodities market, then you know gold has been on fire. It’s up more than 3% in the past five days, 15% in the past three months, and 33% year-to-date! And I’ve been doing my best to help you profit, keeping you updated on the move in Chart of the Week articles like this one from August.

Now, check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Week, which shows silver futures over the last 15 years. You can see silver is going bonkers to the upside and threatening to break out above resistance from 2012-2013. If it does, that could open the door for a move back to the all-time high around $49 an ounce from 2011.

At last week’s 2024 MoneyShow Orlando, I moderated a panel featuring Peter Schiff of Euro Pacific Asset Management, Brien Lundin of Gold Newsletter, and Adrian Day of Global Analyst. They all said this move is the real deal, and that forces like ballooning government debt and deficits... Federal Reserve rate cuts... and a falling dollar will drive silver sharply higher. Hopefully you’re already using the specific strategies and investments they named to profit.

Couldn’t join us? Then you can still profit from buying silver coins, bars, or bullion directly. One of our exhibitors brought this 1,001.2-ounce silver bar to the show, in fact. It’s worth quite a bit now, given the surge in silver (though I could barely move the thing, since it weighs so much!)

If you’d rather not throw your back out, you can buy ETF proxies like the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) instead. Or you can target silver mining companies individually or through an ETF like the Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL).

Regardless of what vehicle you choose, you should enjoy some handsome gains if this move in silver has legs. And I have every reason to believe it does!

data.title: Zscaler's Valuation Woes: A Case For Selling
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Sakshi Agarwal as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

I'm bearish on Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) for several reasons. First, the company is experiencing decelerating growth, with projected revenue growth slowing to around 20.5% for fiscal 2025, significantly down from previous years' rates. This slowdown makes me concerned about Zscaler's ability to maintain momentum in a competitive market. Additionally, Zscaler's stated TAM of $96 billion has an excessively high reliance on execution for upselling add-on products rather than the core endpoint security solution - this according to me is a dire execution risk; if the sales team fails to effectively cross-sell or upsell, revenue growth could further stagnate. Moreover, the steep valuation of Zscaler's stock, trading at approximately 60x expected adjusted earnings or 10x forward P/S, makes it vulnerable to market corrections, especially as growth slows. The increasing competition from larger cloud vendors like Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) could also hinder Zscaler's market share and profitability. Finally, the overall economic environment adds uncertainty, making it challenging for Zscaler to sustain its previous growth trajectory. These factors collectively contribute to my cautious outlook on the stock.

Business Overview

Zscaler is a cloud security company that provides the "Zscaler Zero Trust Exchange" platform, which acts like a secure gateway for end users/applications to access the internet.

Founded in 2007, Zscaler aims to drive digital transformation through its "Zero-Trust platform" which offers an alternative to the legacy VPN and firewall-based network solution. Zscaler primarily targets enterprise customers of all sizes, surpassing $2 billion in annual recurring revenue ARR in FY2023.

Demystifying Secure Network Access

Traditionally, Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) have been used by organizations to allow remote users to securely access corporate data. A VPN operates by creating an encrypted "tunnel" or a "secure gateway" between the user's device and the corporate network. Traditionally, VPNs require on-prem installations, including physical servers and hardware at the organization's location. Employees within an organization connect to the on-prem VPN, which securely routes traffic across the corporate network. It should be clear by now that every large internet organization will have fixed OpEx to provide a secure gateway to corporate access.

However, there are two primary short-comings of the VPN model - (1) Traditional VPNs require on-prem setup and management (2) VPNs offer limited security once a connection is established- through compromised VPN credentials or a vulnerable endpoint, bad actors can move laterally within the network.

Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) offers a new paradigm to provide secure gateway access, aimed at alleviating the limitations of a classical VPN. Firstly, ZTNA is a cloud-native offering, thereby removing the need to set up and maintain a VPN on-prem. Secondly, ZTNA operates differently from VPNs by adhering to a "zero trust" security model. Rather than granting access to an entire corporate network through a single endpoint, ZTNA ensures that users are only granted access to specific applications or resources based on their identity, role, and security posture.

Zscaler's Core Product Offerings

The entire suite of offerings from Zscaler is built around the "Zscaler Zero Trust Exchange" platform. The Zscaler platform is a network of 150 WW data centers which act as intermediate end-users/applications and secure networks or the internet. Once a client signs up with Zscaler, all network requests are routed to the Zscaler network platform where each request is accessed for security threats, and thereafter, the client request is finally routed to the end application or the internet endpoint. This way, the Zscaler network acts as a toll booth for all incoming and outgoing network requests.

The two primary product offerings built on top of the Zscaler network are -

  1. Zscaler Internet Access (ZIA): A secure web gateway that provides safe internet access to users, regardless of location.

  2. Zscaler Private Access (ZPA): A secure connection to corporate applications, whether they are located on-premises or in the cloud.

Value Realization through Cross-Selling

Recall that I mentioned that since all network requests/responses are routed through the Zscaler network. Per the FYQ4 2024 press release, Zscaler processes over 500B transactions per day - which is 20x the number of Google searches performed every day. Consequently, it's clear that Zscaler collects an unparalleled amount of data from its clients which provides an opportunity to upsell product offerings.

Zscaler is a strong believer in the "Land and Expand" strategy and has a broad range of peripheral offerings to expand its Average Revenue per User (ARPU). ARPU is a key metric for any subscription-based service and as seen in recent investor presentations, Zscaler aims to expand ARPU to $175 per user and $155 per workflow through bundles which offer threat analytics, performance metrics, data loss prevention in addition to securing networks.

Is $96 billion TAM an illusion?

According to Grand View Research, the global market for virtual private networks (VPNs) was valued at $48 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow to $137 billion by 2030. I believe that Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) providers like Zscler will continue to take market share away from legacy VPN providers. However, enterprise digital transformation (similar to the on-prem server racks to the cloud) tends to be very slow, and I believe this trend will play over in minute increments over the upcoming decade.

What surprises me is that during the recent investor meeting, Zscaler reported an expansion in its TAM to $96 billion based on high ARPU realization from potential upsell/cross-selling opportunities. If you were to believe that the core offering from Zscaler is a secure gateway, which is an alternative to traditional VPNs, the TAM of the core offering should be ~ $50 billion. This implies that the TAM for Zscaler cross-selling opportunities, which include analytics and business insights, is 100% of its core offering. I'm not trying to disregard the breadth of Zscaler's offerings, but I would like to caution investors about the improbability that Zscaler would be able to grab sizable revenue in the non-core TAM segment. My research does not point me to specific revenue targets Zscaler has published for non-core offerings like ZDX, Data-Loss Protection (DLP), Risk360, and Business Insights (BI). This leads me to believe that Zscaler is yet to make significant inroads in these product offerings and revenue realization from the projected TAM expansion hinges on a successful execution.

Decelerating Growth

ZS's revenue has been steadily declining for the last eight quarters, to a growth rate of 30% in the most recent quarter. Additionally, the FY2025 revenue growth outlook is projected to further decline to 20.5% - owing to declining billings. In my opinion, poor billings are particularly concerning, as it indicates that Zscaler is struggling to onboard new customers or grab market share away from existing providers.

Decelerating growth is further exacerbated by increasing competition, with both established players and emerging companies vying for market share. The entry of larger companies into the Zero Trust security like Microsoft with Defender and PANW with Prisma Cloud poses stiff competition to Zscaler.

Another factor contributing to decelerating revenue is the decline in Net Retention Rate (NRR). While Zscaler previously boasted a dollar-based NRR consistently above 125%, it has experienced a decline in the recent three quarters to 116%. Zscaler's management attributes the NRR decline partly to a strategic shift towards selling larger product bundles. While Zscaler's management attributes declining NRR to changing sales strategy, I believe declining NRR could signal deteriorating customer engagement. It's no secret that SaaS contracts are heavily marked-down to land new customers and as contracts expire, I believe it's possible that customers may choose against renewing in favor of exploring competing products or probably because the core value proposition was not strong enough.

Valuation

At a forward P/S of 10 and a forward non-GAAP P/E of 60, Zscaler is not cheap. Compare this to other cybersecurity SaaS companies like Okta, Inc. (OKTA) which trade at a forward P/S of 5 and a forward non-GAAP P/E of 30. Zscaler is also not profitable on a GAAP basis due to the impact of stock-based compensation. Zscaler incurs substantial ($527.7 million in FY24) Stock-Based Compensation (SBC) expenses as part of its employee compensation structure.

In estimating a fair value for Zscaler, I employed a reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model, which enables us to extract the growth expectations that the market has factored into the current stock price. I focused on discounting Zscaler's free cash flow (FCF) over a five-year period. This five-year period is suitable because the cybersecurity industry is fast-changing, with quick technological advancements and shifting customer needs that can greatly affect financial performance. For my bull case scenario, I assumed a terminal value growth rate of 2%. I believe that a 2% terminal growth rate is reasonable for a tech company in a mature industry, aligning with broader economic growth expectations. In projecting revenue growth, I applied a gradient starting from 20% and tapering down to 15% over the five-year period. This assumption is supported by Zscaler's recent performance, where it reported a 30% year-over-year revenue increase in its latest quarter and anticipates continued growth in the range of 20% to 21% for fiscal year 2025. I also considered an operating margin gradient ranging from 20% to 25%. This range is justified by Zscaler's recent financial results, which indicate an improvement in non-GAAP operating income margins-rising from 15% to 20%

Based on my discounted cash flow analysis, I estimate Zscaler, Inc.'s (ZS) fair value at $116. Given the current market price of ~$190, I maintain a Sell rating on the stock, as it appears significantly overvalued by approximately 63%. However, I would revise my rating to a Hold, if the stock price were to decline to $160. At this level, while still above my fair value estimate, the valuation premium would be more reasonable considering Zscaler's strong market position and growth potential in the cloud security sector.

Risks to my thesis

Some key risks to my thesis include (1) Strong adoption of Zero-Trust architecture: The entire cyber-security market is seeing a secular trend toward the adoption of Zero-Trust architecture-based solutions. Zscaler, despite facing strong competition, has a first mover advantage in the segment and could potentially retain and grab market share from legacy players (2) Value unlocking to cross-selling opportunities: Zscaler, as a key intermediary in securing cloud and internet traffic, sits at a unique vantage point with access to vast amounts of network and security data. Contingent to execution, this dynamic can help drive long-term value by increasing customer stickiness and boosting ARPU.

Verdict and Conclusion

The cybersecurity industry is seeing a secular trend towards the adoption of Zero-Trust solutions, and Zscaler is at the heart of the digital transformation. However, digital transformation tends to be sluggish and plays out over decades. Zscaler's market capitalization (at $30 billion) is already half that of traditional VPN network providers, yet it has nowhere near the customer base or scale of these established players. With a decelerating revenue growth and declining NRR, such a premium valuation can only be justified if Zscaler successfully capitalizes on its cross-selling and upselling opportunities, making significant inroads into products like threat analytics and data-loss protection. With no data around Zscaler's effectiveness in the newer opportunities, steep valuation, competition headwinds, and lack of GAAP profitability, I recommend a SELL on Zscaler at the current market cap of $30 billion.

data.title: Comstock May Be Taken Private In A Gas Bear Market
data.description:
There is chatter on the street that Comstock Resources, Inc. (NYSE:CRK) will be taken private by Jerry Jones as announced on Seeking Alpha on September 20, 2024. This investment is an addition to his $1.1b investment in CRK shares. According to the report, the remaining float was valued at $851mm, creating an appealing opportunity to the investor to buy out the remaining shares.

The market dynamics for Comstock have significantly changed in the past two years as natural gas prices cratered with supply outstripping demand. I believe Comstock’s plan to go all-in on the Haynesville/Bossier shale assets was intended to take advantage of the geographical location for the growing LNG export capacity along the Gulf Coast. As turbulence hit the market throughout 2024 with the pause on additional export capacity, the value of Comstock’s assets came into question as the Haynesville/Bossier shale is a higher D&C basin when compared to the Marcellus shale. Given the buyout opportunity at hand and the challenging macroeconomic environment, I rate CRK shares with a HOLD rating. Assuming no buyout occurs, my price target for CRK shares is $4.93/share at 6x TTM EV/EBITDA.

Comstock Operations

As of Q2 ’24, Comstock had over 1,400 net drilling locations available, representing 30 years of drilling activity. Comstock also acquired 450,000 net acres in the Western Haynesville area that is expected to ease drilling and completion costs, a major differentiator when comparing Haynesville production to Marcellus production. In Q2 ’24, Comstock drilled 11 wells in the Haynesville/Bossier shale and has turned 15 new wells to sales with an average initial production rate in the range of 10-31MMcf/d. Total production for the quarter summed to 1.4Bcfe/day, up 4% from Q2 ’23.

Comstock dropped 2 rigs in Q1 ’24 and is currently running 5 rigs. The firm is running 2 frac crews, down from 3 at the beginning of the year. The firm has also furloughed 1 of the frac crews, intending to pick it back up in Q4 ’24.

Comstock invested $221mm on development activities in Q2 ’24, all of which was spent developing its Haynesville & Bossier shale assets.

Comstock Resources Financial Position

The natural gas market certainly has not been appealing for the gas pure play, as the average selling price has continued to decline since the European natural gas shortage in 2022. The average, unhedged selling price in Q2 ’24 fell -9% to $1.65/Mcf. This has placed tremendous pressure on operations with revenue from natural gas sales declining by -5% and total revenue, including gas services, declining by -14% in Q2 ’24. This has also placed pressure on margins, with unadjusted EBITDA dropping to 41%.

Drilling & completion costs experienced an uplift in Q2 ’24, averaging $1,730/ft, representing a 15% sequential increase. Drilling costs increased sequentially by 31% to $936/ft while completion costs increased by 1% to $794/ft in the quarter. The higher drilling costs were in part impacted by drilling difficulties with its Baker three well pad in the Lake Bistineau area. Management anticipates D&C costs to normalize over the coming quarters.

Mapping out the remaining two quarters of eFY24, I’m forecasting revenue to come in at $375mm & $383mm for Q3 ’24 & Q4 ’24 with a diluted EPS of -$0.10/share & $0.04/share, respectively.

Comstock’s plan will begin playing out in 2h25 as more LNG export capacity comes online. This may come with some pricing headwinds as international gas assets are further developed with a focus on export capacity. Qatar will be growing its LNG capacity from 77MTPA to 142MTPA by 2030. Though domestic LNG exports will have their place in the market, the flood of new capacity can potentially hamper the price dispersion between domestic and international sales.

In total, Comstock has $1.2b in liquidity with no maturities through 2029. In April 2024, Comstock issued $400mm in 2029 notes and used the proceeds to pay down its bank credit facility. The firm’s leverage position is 3.99x net debt/EBITDA, a relatively high level for the firm’s recent history. Net debt has grown 30% from the previous year in Q2 ’24 while EBITDA has declined by -17%.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

CRK shares currently trade at a significant premium to its peer natural gas producers at 8.65x TTM EV/EBITDA. Considering the macroeconomic risk relating to natural gas export and pricing pressures, I have reason to believe that CRK shares will experience downward pressure and may remediate to the average trading premium of 6x EV/EBITDA. This would result in a share price of $4.93/share.

Given the challenging gas environment, management has elected to suspend the quarterly dividend, saving $140mm in annualized payments. Given Jerry Jones’s $100mm investment in Q2 ’24 in addition to his previous ~$1.1b investment, this may be a precursor to the company being taken private.

With the current state of the natural gas market and the possibility for a buyout, I recommend Comstock Resources, Inc. stock with a HOLD rating. Net of the buyout opportunity, I believe CRK shares should be valued at $4.93/share. If a buyout were to occur, CRK would likely be taken off the market at a premium to its current trading level.

data.title: UPS Q3 Earnings Preview: Volume Mix And Recent Acquisitions Should Be In Focus
data.description:
Introduction

United Parcel Service, Inc. (NYSE:UPS) is scheduled to release its Q3 earnings on Thursday, October 24. In this article, I investigate the key developments that investors need to watch out for in the company’s Q3 report.

A Recap of United Parcel Service's Q2 Results

UPS delivered a disappointing Q2, with both revenues and EPS missing analyst estimates. More specifically, Q2 revenues came in at $21.82 billion, down 1.1% y/y and missing analyst estimates by $418.5 million. Diluted EPS of $1.79, came in much worse. It was not only down 29.5% y/y but also missed analyst estimates by $0.20. While the EPS news was not surprising, given that the company’s new labor contracts were expected to be a drag on earnings, the revenue surprise was unexpected, as they fell short of the company’s low-end of the guidance. Both operating profits and operating margins were disappointed.

Management’s updated guidance was also hardly encouraging. While FY24 revenue projections of $93 billion came in slightly above analysts' expectations of $92.8 billion, the consolidated adjusted operating margin projection of 9.4% was disappointing in my opinion. UPS did have some encouraging news for income-loving investors, as it declared an unexpected repurchase program of approximately $500 million.

Q3 Expectations

For the third quarter, according to LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), revenues are expected to come in, on average, at $22.14 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 5.13%. Diluted EPS is expected to come in at $1.63, which represents a y/y growth of 3.59%.

The company, according to LSEG data, has a terrible track record when it comes to meeting revenue expectations, as evidenced by the fact that it has missed revenue estimates in eight of the last ten quarters. Moreover, it has been eight consecutive quarters of revenue misses by the company, with an average negative surprise of 1.93%.

Post the second quarter report, the stock fell 10.2%, and this is despite the company attempting to attract income-loving investors with a surprise repurchase program. It was one of the steepest declines in the recent past. All Eyes Should be on Volume Mix & Impact from Port Strikes

During the last quarter, the company did cite the trade-down by customers to lower-priced services in the US, as the main reason that adversely affected its profitability. More specifically, previous Air customers opted for Ground, and previous Ground customers opted for SurePost in the US, which resulted in a significant decline in ADV for Air Segment and the low-cost SurePost saw its volumes surge 25% y/y.

Management has already warned that this trend is expected to continue for the rest of 2024. However, to what extent has this situation progressed is an area that investors need to look out for in the Q3 report. Should there be more volume expansion of SurePost and lower volume expansion for Air segment, then the company is guaranteed to miss its profitability guidance? In fact, I wouldn’t be surprised if the management revises the guidance downward, if the trend has gotten worse.

One positive development, from UPS’s perspective, is the port strike, which took place during this quarter. The strike, launched by the International Longshoremen’s Association workers union and the US Maritime Alliance port operators, led to a massive work stoppage and affected 36 ports from Maine to Texas. While the port strikes were thankfully resolved in three days, I would be curious to know whether the Air division of UPS saw some benefits. I would also be interested to hear from the management whether the impact of the port strikes, albeit short-lived, led to a boost in volumes for the company’s more costly Air segment. This could have subsequently led to more deals for the Air division during the upcoming holiday season. A meaningful positive impact from the port strikes could help UPS’s bottom-line and while it may not reverse the trend of trade-downs, it would at least prevent it from worsening it. This is an area, therefore, that investors should watch out, during the earnings call.

UPS’s Acquisition Spree: What It Means for Long-Term Shareholders

The last time I wrote about UPS, I touched upon UPS’s announcement of its acquisition of Estafeta, a leading Mexican express delivery company. The acquisition is expected to close by the end of the year. In my article, I talked about how the acquisition can allow UPS to take advantage of the nearshoring boom, although I questioned whether Mexico was ready to capitalize on the nearshoring boom.

Then, last month, the company announced that it was acquiring Frigo-Trans and its sister company BPL for an undisclosed amount. These companies are healthcare logistics providers based in Germany, and the company disclosed that the acquisition would allow it to enhance its end-to-end capabilities throughout Europe for the company’s healthcare customers. The transaction is expected to close in the first quarter of 2025.

From a growth perspective, once again, the acquisition makes sense. According to Market Data Forecast, the healthcare logistics market in Europe is expected to grow to $43.92 billion by 2029, which would represent a CAGR of 6.95% from 2024 to 2029. However, there are question marks again.

First, the transaction is expected to close in the first quarter of 2025, which means it won’t have any impact on the revenues and EPS in FY24. Second, the value and terms of the transaction were undisclosed. I would expect one or two analyst questions on the deal value, since it would give a clearer picture on how the company is financing the deal. If you recall correctly, the surprise $500 million in share repurchases that was announced by the company, was because the company was tightening its capital expenditure.

For a company that is taking such measures, the capital allocation, to me, remains a bit confusing. The company has announced three acquisitions already in the past three months, and given that FY24 is likely to be a challenging one, there needs to be clarity on the rationale and the payment of these acquisitions. As such, investors need to watch out for any additional management commentary on the latest acquisitions, and what the M&A strategy going forward will be.

UPS Stock Valuation

UPS, according to LSEG Data, currently trades at a forward P/E of 15.7x, which makes it pricier than its main rival FedEx Corp, which trades at 13.14x.

The stock is also not trading far off from its historical levels. For instance, its 5-year historical median multiple is 16.2x and its 10-year historical median forward P/E multiple is 16.7x. So, on a historical basis, the stock is fairly valued, which, in my opinion, makes it even more risky to get into the name before the earnings release, especially given its unimpressive history of missing expectations.

The last time I wrote about UPS, I had a price target of $113 because of a forward P/E multiple of 14.8x and a projected FY25 EPS of $7.65. In the same article, I projected FY24 EPS to come in at $7.22. Even if the company misses its EPS expectations in Q3, it is still on track to match my FY24 EPS target. This subsequently lends support to the notion that it would match its FY25 EPS targets.

Assuming the fair P/E multiple of 15.7x (higher than my prior estimate of 14.8x) and a projected FY25 EPS of $7.65, the price target would be $120 (vs. $113). Despite raising the price target, it still suggests a downside of about 11.7% from current levels. Given the history of the company’s stock reaction around earnings, and given that there is further downside even at current levels, I am maintaining my SELL rating on the stock. However, my stance remains unchanged going into the earnings, which is that I wouldn’t recommend shorting the stock. However, existing investors can book profits at current levels in my opinion, especially before the earnings release.

Concluding Thoughts

UPS does not have a good track record of beating EPS or Revenue estimates. As such, going into its Q3 report, investors need to be cautious. The key focus would be on its volume mix and whether the recent port strikes had any positive impact on its Air division. I would also be watching out for any commentary and clarity on the company’s recent acquisitions of German healthcare logistics providers. More specifically, I would be looking out for the terms of the deal, especially how the company plans to finance the acquisition, at a time when it is going through a challenging phase.

From a valuation perspective, UPS remains overvalued in my opinion. As such, I would not be buying into the name, especially before the report. It would also be not a bad idea, in my opinion, if investors trim their existing stakes. There are a lot of question marks surrounding the company, but the main question is how a company that announced curtailing its capital expenditures suddenly pivots to being a serial acquirer. How these moves would affect long-term investors would be what needs to be looked into when the company announces its earnings on Thursday.

data.title: TLTW ETF: Maybe It's Time To Accumulate
data.description:
I hate to say this, but in my opinion, now is a good time to start accumulating the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (BATS:TLTW). As you may already know, I'm not a big fan of Buy-Write ETFs, and in a previous article, I explained why. However, now, especially for TLTW, I genuinely think it could provide a good hedge for our fixed-rate portfolio (or as part of a well-diversified portfolio).

The reason is that the macroeconomic scenario, heavily influenced by the upcoming election, has shifted. I'm now concerned about my exposure to the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

What do I mean? I mean that the steepening process could be more pronounced than earlier projections suggested. I believe that 20+ year Treasury bonds may trade sideways for an extended period, while the spread between other passive income solutions widens.

What is TLTW, and what drives the price of this asset?

TLTW is a buy-write ETF, and its technical mechanism is well presented in the fund profile on Seeking Alpha

The investment seeks to track the investment results of the Cboe TLT 2% OTM BuyWrite Index that reflects a strategy of holding the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF while writing (selling) one-month call options to generate income.

If you want to learn more about buy-write ETFs, I recommend reading this article.

Currently, the Yield (FWD) is around 15.34%, with an expense ratio of 0.35%. Focusing on the first statistic, which in my opinion is the most important when analyzing this ETF, we can say that BlackRock uses the rolling method very effectively, and the yield history has maintained a certain level of stability.

Naturally, the yield is not fixed and changes primarily due to several critical factors:

Firstly, it's quite interesting to note that there is no clear parallelism between the % yield growth of TLT and TLTW. This is because the % yield growth of TLTW depends on the price of TLT. When the yield of TLT increases, the price of the underlying asset decreases, and the covered call strategy generates more profit, which causes the yield of TLTW to increase as well. This becomes more evident when we compare the price return of TLT with the % change in the yield of TLTW.

Indeed, the monthly roll-up of the covered call strategy had a negative impact toward the end of 2023 and the start of 2024. The price of TLT started to rise suddenly and significantly due to changes in the market's expectations of Fed rate cuts. In summary, a sudden positive reaction in the price of TLT can severely affect the returns of TLTW.

But there's another crucial point: the spread between the price of TLT and TLTW started to widen at the end of 2024 when the price of TLT began to rise due to increasing rate cut projections. This reflects the cost of the covered call strategy, which is likely to become more burdensome whenever there is a sudden and positive change in the price of TLT.

What is happening to TLT and TLTW?

Theoretically, what moves the price of TLTW? That would be BlackRock's scenario analysis.

This is an important table to keep in mind. If rates fall, as we've already shown, TLTW might not be the best choice, and the Fed has already begun the process of lowering the fed rate. But are you paying attention to what's happening with over 20-year Treasuries?

While the rate cuts are ongoing…

The yield on long-duration U.S. Treasuries is increasing-just look at the U.S. 30-year yield from September to October.

And that has caused a decrease in TLT.

Why is that happening? Naturally, it's due to the steepening process. In fact, this hasn't affected Treasuries with shorter maturities, such as the three-year U.S. Treasury (3USY).

Just remember that longer-duration bonds are more sensitive to changes in yields, and investors should not make the mistake of thinking that a Fed rate cut will necessarily affect the entire yield curve.

Why accumulate TLTW

So why would you accumulate TLTW now?

In my opinion, it's because there won't be a significant decrease in over 20-year Treasury yields as many are expecting.

So, what's happening? The steepening process is in effect, and the yield curve is normalizing, naturally helped by the U.S. macroeconomic situation. While it's likely that interest rates will be lower in the short term, is that equally true in the long term? I don't know for sure, and neither does the market. In my opinion, there's a potential obstacle to the widespread strategy of buying long-duration bonds to benefit from Fed rate cuts: the U.S. election. I don't want to delve too deeply into this, as it's not a political issue, but whether it's Harris or Trump, with either an increase in spending or a cut in taxes, the public deficit will likely increase. The money supply (M2) will exceed previous highs, and long-term expectations for GDP growth and inflation (over 20 years) will remain higher than in the short term, just like they were in the past four years, keeping long-term interest rates elevated.

So, to hedge my TLT exposure and take advantage of a higher yield, I'm considering taking a position in TLTW now.

Risk

And I know that's risky because, as you've already seen, the spread between TLTW and TLT widens when TLT increases suddenly. So, a drastic shift in the long-term macroeconomic outlook could lead to a significant price drop for TLTW, as well as a decline in yield. But let's take a look at the total return comparison between TLTW and TLT.

If we consider this as a chart comparison during a Treasury bull market, I think the 'opportunity cost' is not unsustainable, with a spread in total return of 6.42%.

Conclusion

To conclude, I like the idea of adding TLTW to my portfolio to benefit from the high yield. I believe this could be a great moment to take a position because the 20+ year Treasury yield may not react as the majority expects. The steepening process of the yield curve, combined with less accommodative long-term expectations, seems to create a fertile ground for a sideways trend in long-duration Treasuries, potentially leading to a reduction in the total return spread between TLT and TLTW.

Review & Outlook

U.S. equities closed higher for a fourth consecutive quarter, as generally upbeat economic reports supporting the soft-landing narrative and the Federal Reserve's long-awaited dovish pivot spurred a meaningful rotation out of large-cap, Magnificent Seven stocks and into value/cyclicals/small caps. Beneath the surface, the market advance was anything but uneventful, with the S&P 500 Index falling by nearly 9% in late July/early August and by more than 4% in early September, before quickly recouping losses on both occasions. Shifting investor sentiment was reflected in the movement of the VIX Index, which briefly spiked above 65, its highest level since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic and a mark hit only a few times in its 35-year history. The Magnificent Seven, which dominated market performance during the first half of the year, moved in opposite directions during the third quarter, as double-digit gains from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) were mostly offset by declines from Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL) , Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), with the group posting a modest gain of 1.7% for the period. Sector leadership was centered in rate-sensitive sectors expected to benefit from lower interest rates, namely Utilities, Real Estate (REITs), Industrials (building products), Financials (regional banks and insurers), and Consumer Discretionary (homebuilders). Information Technology and Communication Services underperformed due to declines from certain members of the Magnificent Seven, specifically Microsoft, Nvidia, and Alphabet.

Despite this backdrop for the quarter, Fund performance was solid, with a strong quarterly absolute gain of just over 4%, and modest relative outperformance versus the Benchmark. This was due to stock picking, as the Fund's selection and interaction effect added nearly 100 basis points of relative gains. Relative outperformance stemmed from our biotechnology investments in argenx SE (ARGX, antibody immunology platform) and Arcellx, Inc. (ACLX, cell therapy for multiple myeloma); our application software investments in Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE, property and casualty (P&C) insurance platform) and Samsara Inc. (IOT, industrial digitization pioneer); electric vehicle (EV) and autonomous driving and robotics leader, Tesla, Inc.; and consumer digital plays Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL, language learning innovator), Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT, streaming audio leader), and Shopify Inc. (SHOP, e-commerce platform). Not owning Alphabet was another positive relative contributor in the quarter. On the flip side, notable relative detractors included our auto- semiconductor holdings, indie Semiconductor, Inc. AI Infrastructure/Semiconductor Demand

Following up on the AI discussion in our last letter,[1] our research over the past three months, including a recent Baron research trip up and down Silicon Valley, has confirmed that we remain in an AI arms race. This arms race can be described as a contest to develop the one foundational model[2] to "rule them all," to borrow a line from Tolkien, and be the first to achieve artificial general intelligence, or AGI.[3] The racecourse has been built on scaling laws, and the race obstacles are data, compute, and models. AI scaling laws describe the predictable trend of improvement in model performance with increases in three key factors: model size (number of parameters), data size (amount of training data), and compute instances (amount of computation used). Here is a simple breakdown:

  • More data: As we train models on larger datasets, they generally perform better. More data provides a richer set of examples for the model to learn from, helping it capture patterns and nuances.
  • More Compute: Increasing computational power (more GPUs or faster processors) allows for training larger models or training existing models more thoroughly. With more compute, models can process data faster and experiment with more parameters, leading to better performance.
  • Larger Models: Increasing the size of the model, i.e., the weights or parameters, helps the model gain more capacity or brainpower to learn from larger datasets and capture more intricate patterns.
  • Better Answers: Larger models provide better answers to user questions.

Companies that don't enter the AI race are left watching from the sidelines, missing out on the biggest technology and industrial transformation and economic jackpot in a generation. The entry fee of capital and operating investments is high. But the winners' podium will likely hold more than just the first, second, and third places, but probably less than ten. None of the large hyperscalers nor consumer internet players, not to mention a handful of sovereigns, can risk not having runners in this race.

To be sure, just the other day, Jensen Huang, Nvidia's CEO, stated in a CNBC interview that "the demand for Blackwell [Nvidia's next-generation platform] is insane. Everybody wants to have the most and everybody wants to be first." Larry Ellison, the CEO and founder of Oracle (ORCL), publicly told the story of how he and Elon Musk were literally "begging Jensen for GPUs" over dinner with him. Elon's xAI recently opened a new data center, called Colossus, in Memphis, Tennessee, which was described by an xAI employee as "the largest data center on the planet." On our research trip, we met with the CEO of a major AI infrastructure player, who stated, in very blunt terms, we are "building…big a** networks for them," explaining that each of the "big 5" - Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Oracle - are planning to build data centers at the scale of 100,000 AI accelerator chips (i.e., GPUs).

Here are a few quotes from hyperscaler CEOs on the AI infrastructure arms race:

  • Amazon CEO Andy Jassy: "While we're investing a significant amount in the AI space and in infrastructure, we would like to have more capacity than we already have today. I mean, we have a lot of demand right now, and I think it's going to be a very, very large business for us."
  • Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg: "The downside of being behind is that you're out of position for like the most important technology for the next 10 to 15 years…I'd rather risk building capacity before it is needed rather than too late, given the long lead times for spinning up new infra projects…The amount of compute needed to train Llama 4 will likely be almost 10 times more than what we used to train Llama 3 and future models will continue to grow beyond that."
  • Alphabet's Sundar Pichai: "When we go through a curve like this, the risk of underinvesting is dramatically greater than the risk of overinvesting for us here. Even in scenarios where if it turns out that we are overinvesting… these are infrastructure which are widely useful for us."

While scaling laws have governed the mounting investments in model training infrastructure, they are now set to drive an inflection in inference compute requirements. Just a few weeks ago, OpenAI released a new series of "chain of thought" models, nicknamed Strawberry and released as the o1 series, designed to spend more time thinking and reasoning before they provide an inference response (measured by the number of tokens). These models reason through complex tasks and solve harder problems than previous models in science, coding, and math. This is a new type of inference architecture, where the models iterate on the answer after the initial prompt or query. In simple terms, the first answer becomes the new prompt and the model cycles through iterations as it "thinks" until the best answer is achieved. Inference innovations and evolutions like this require significantly more compute for inferencing, another growth driver for the AI accelerator manufacturers, like Nvidia and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) One of our team asked Jensen about this and he replied that "this is a massive inflection…all of a sudden, the model has to be inferenced many more times for each token… depending on the quality of the answer." A recent Morgan Stanley note stated: "inference is starting to solve much more complex problems which will require a richer mix of hardware...the notion that a more thoughtful, task-oriented inference would cause an exponential jump in inference complexity strikes us as an important new avenue for growth."

The Data Layer

In our discussion of software in our last letter,[4] we stated that "the enterprise software winners will have to be better at delivering AI services and features than build-your-own AI tools" and that "the winners will be the ones that have a well-established product development culture of innovation and iteration; differentiated proprietary, industry, and customer data; distribution advantages with large customer bases, successful go-to-market efforts, and key partners; well-designed workflows where AI improves the user interface, intelligent predictions/recommendations, and automation; and established always-on connectivity and feedback from their customers; among other things." In this piece, we'd like to focus on the importance of the "the data layer" of the AI stack and in building differentiated AI applications.

While much attention today is often placed on the semiconductor or infrastructure layer of the AI stack, as we did above, our research has confirmed that it is the data layer - proprietary data and robust data processing - that forms the foundation for advanced AI applications. To build transformative, reliable AI applications, companies will need to build strategies around their data. Without a robust and scalable data infrastructure, AI models and algorithms lack essential raw material to generate meaningful insights, predictions, and automation. An advantageous data asset is not just about volume, it's about how the data is managed and processed - it's about quality (is the data accurate, complete, clean, and reliable?), structure (how is the data organized, stored, and manipulated?), accessibility (can models access it for inference and training?), orchestration (how is the data organized and combined with data from multiple sources?), and governance (is the data compliant and secure?). Proprietary datasets are becoming key differentiators. Organizations with unique access to high-quality, domain-specific data are positioning themselves to build smaller, use-case-specific models that deliver superior performance, enabling them to solve more complex problems. For example, Samsara is accelerating its market share capture in the telematics and commercial fleet safety sector because of the 10 trillion data points it collects from the more than 70 billion miles driven annually. The growing data asset - which is cleaned, structured, and stored in one connected operation cloud - fuels more AI-powered insights that help reduce accidents, emissions, and insurance premiums, driving more value than competitive solutions. Datadog, Inc. (DDOG) monitors the IT infrastructure and application activity for 28,700 customers in every industry, collecting trillions of data points per hour from a very diverse set of application types and IT environments. It then applies AI techniques like root cause analysis, natural language querying, and autonomous investigations to help customers solve IT problems faster than competing solutions. Looking forward, we believe companies that have a firm grasp on data infrastructure, combined with unique access to proprietary datasets, will be essential drivers of value in the AI ecosystem.
